candidates.label: Real Estate Operator
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Operator is a professional or entity responsible for the day-to-day management, maintenance, and operations of real estate properties. This role includes overseeing property functions, ensuring tenant satisfaction, handling leasing activities, and maintaining the physical condition of the property.

candidates.label: Real Estate Investor
candidates.definition: Entities or individuals that invest in real property, either directly or through a real estate investment trust (REIT).

candidates.label: Real Estate Developer
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Developer is a professional or company that acquires land, finances real estate deals, builds or renovates properties, and arranges for their sale or lease. This role involves various stages of development, including market research, site selection, obtaining permits, and managing construction projects.

candidates.label: Realtor
candidates.definition: From U.S. Bankruptcy Court PACER

candidates.definition: Real estate asset type

candidates.label: Estate
candidates.definition: An Estate refers to all the possessions, property, and legal rights owned by an individual, including land, tenements, and personal assets. It also encompasses the total assets and liabilities left by a person at death, which are subject to distribution according to the terms of a will or the laws of intestacy.

candidates.label: Real Estate Documents
candidates.definition: Real Estate Documents are written or recorded representations of property ownership, transfer, or use, including but not limited to deeds, mortgages, and leases.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Real Estate Lease
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Lease is a contract wherein the owner (lessor) grants the right to occupy and use their property to another party (lessee) for a specified period in exchange for rental payments.

candidates.label: Real Estate Sublease
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Sublease is an agreement where the original tenant (sublessor) rents out the property to another tenant (sublessee), either for the entirety or a portion of the remaining lease term.

candidates.label: Real Estate Commission
candidates.definition: A real estate commission is a fee paid to real estate agents or brokers for their services in facilitating the sale or purchase of property. It is typically a percentage of the final sale price and compensates the agent for their efforts in marketing, negotiating, and closing the real estate transaction

candidates.label: Real Estate Industry
candidates.definition: Industries in the Real Estate group are primarily engaged in renting or leasing real estate to others; managing real estate for others; selling, buying, or renting real estate for others; and providing other real estate related services, such as appraisal services. This subsector includes equity Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) that are primarily engaged in leasing buildings, dwellings, or other real estate property to others.

hierarchy_path.label: Real Estate, Rental and Leasing Industry

candidates.label: Land Settlement
candidates.definition: Land settlement refers to the process where individuals or groups occupy and establish a community or settlement on a specific tract of land, often for agricultural, residential, or developmental purposes. This concept can encompass the historical settling of new territories, as well as modern real estate transactions where land is allocated and developed.

candidates.label: Disinheritance
candidates.definition: Disinheritance is the legal act of deliberately excluding an heir from receiving property or assets from a deceased person's estate, typically through the terms specified in a will or trust. This action can be taken for various personal, financial, or relational reasons, and must comply with legal statutes to be effective.

candidates.label: Criminal Trespass
candidates.definition: To unlawfully enter land, a dwelling, or other real property.

candidates.label: Bequest
candidates.definition: A Bequest is a provision in a will or trust that designates the transfer of specific assets, property, or money to a particular beneficiary upon the death of the testator. It ensures that the intended recipients receive the designated items as part of the estate distribution.

candidates.label: Real Estate Fraud
candidates.definition: Real Estate Fraud: A type of fraud claim that involves deceitful or dishonest practices related to the buying, selling, or leasing of real property, which may result in property or premises liability claims.

candidates.label: Trespass to Real Property
candidates.definition: The concept of Trespass to Real Property refers to the intentional and unauthorized entry onto someone else's land or property, causing damage or interference with the owner's right to exclusive possession.

candidates.label: Turnover of property to the estate
candidates.definition: The turnover of property to the estate refers to the legal process of transferring ownership or possession of assets to a bankruptcy estate, typically as a result of an avoidance action claim.

candidates.label: Occupancy
candidates.definition: Occupancy refers to the act of residing in or using a property or space. This includes the possession and control of the property, typically under a lease or ownership agreement, and entails responsibilities such as maintenance and adherence to property regulations. Occupancy refers to the physical act of living in or using a property, which can be temporary or intermittent, in contrast to "residence," which refers to a person's fixed, permanent, and principal home, often with legal implications and the intention to remain for an extended period.

candidates.label: Commercial Transactions Law
candidates.definition: Law related to business contracts and agreements.

candidates.label: Personal Property Law
candidates.definition: Matters relating to personal property — not real property — such as damage to vehicles, ownership of valuables (e.g., jewelry), trespass to chattels, etc.

candidates.label: Estate
candidates.definition: An Estate refers to all the possessions, property, and legal rights owned by an individual, including land, tenements, and personal assets. It also encompasses the total assets and liabilities left by a person at death, which are subject to distribution according to the terms of a will or the laws of intestacy.

candidates.label: Property Rights
candidates.definition: Property rights refer to the legal entitlements associated with the ownership, use, and disposition of property, including land, buildings, and other immovable assets. These rights generally include the ability to possess, control, lease, sell, and bequeath the property, subject to certain limitations imposed by law

candidates.label: Abandonment of Property
candidates.definition: Escheatment is the legal process by which unclaimed or abandoned property reverts to the state. This occurs when the rightful owner of the property cannot be located or has failed to claim ownership within a specified period, resulting in the transfer of the property to government custody.

candidates.label: Nonpossessory Interest
candidates.definition: Nonpossessory Interest refers to a type of property interest that grants certain rights to use or enjoy the property without possessing it. This includes interests such as easements, covenants, and profits à prendre, where the holder has limited rights related to the property owned by another person.

candidates.label: Property Disparagement
candidates.definition: The act of making false or misleading statements about someone's property, which causes harm to their reputation or financial interests, and can result in legal action under the categories of defamation, property, and premises liability claims.

candidates.label: Transactional Practice
candidates.definition: Any process that involves two or more parties in creation of a contract.
synonyms: トランザクション実務

candidates.label: Settlement Practice
candidates.definition: Settlement Practice refers to the legal process of negotiating and reaching an agreement between parties involved in a legal dispute, often involving a demand for compensation or other remedy.

candidates.label: Funds Practice
candidates.definition: Funds Practice involves the legal aspects of forming, managing, and regulating investment funds, including private equity funds, hedge funds, and venture capital funds. This practice area covers fund structuring, compliance with financial regulations, investor relations, and transactional activities.

candidates.label: Tax Practice
candidates.definition: Tax practice refers lawyers assisting individuals or organizations in navigating tax laws, including compliance, filing returns, and handling disputes with tax authorities. These lawyers may also advise on tax planning strategies to optimize tax liability and benefit

candidates.label: Estate Administration Practice
candidates.definition: Estate Administration Practice refers to the process of managing and settling an individual's estate after their death, including distributing assets to beneficiaries, paying debts, and handling legal and tax obligations. It involves probate proceedings, inventorying assets, and ensuring the deceased's wishes as outlined in their will are fulfilled.

candidates.label: Estate Management Practice
candidates.definition: Estate Management Practice refers to the administration and oversight of a person's estate, including assets, liabilities, and financial affairs, both during their lifetime and after their death. It encompasses tasks such as estate planning, executing wills, managing trusts, and ensuring compliance with legal and tax obligations.

candidates.label: Estate Planning Practice
candidates.definition: Estate Planning Practice involves the preparation and arrangement of an individual's estate to manage and distribute assets during their lifetime and after death. It includes creating legal documents such as wills, trusts, and powers of attorney to ensure that an individual's wishes are followed and to minimize taxes and legal complications.

candidates.label: Copyright Management
candidates.definition: Copyright Management refers to the process of managing and protecting the exclusive rights granted to the owner of an original work, such as a literary, musical, or artistic creation, including the right to reproduce, distribute, and display the work.

candidates.label: Property Rights
candidates.definition: Property rights refer to the legal entitlements associated with the ownership, use, and disposition of property, including land, buildings, and other immovable assets. These rights generally include the ability to possess, control, lease, sell, and bequeath the property, subject to certain limitations imposed by law

candidates.label: Property Right of Use
candidates.definition: The Right of Property Use refers to the civil law concept of legal entitlement to utilize a property for certain activities as permitted by law or contract. This can include residential, commercial, or agricultural uses, and may be subject to zoning laws, covenants, and other legal restrictions

candidates.label: Property Right to Profit
candidates.definition: In civil law systems, the Right to Profit ("droit de prendre") refers to a real right that allows an individual or entity to enter another's land to extract resources like minerals, timber, or fish. This right is usually granted through a contract or license and is subject to the terms and conditions specified therein, as well as overarching legal regulations.

candidates.label: Property Surface Right
candidates.definition: Property Surface Right refers to the legal entitlement to use and control the surface of a piece of land, but not the air above it or the minerals below it. This right is often relevant in land leasing situations, such as when the subsurface rights are leased for mineral extraction while the surface rights are used for agricultural or residential purposes

candidates.label: Property Right of Domain
candidates.definition: In civil law jurisdictions, the "Right of Domain" usually refers to the principle that the state has the ultimate ownership over all land within its territory. This concept allows the government to expropriate private property for public use, typically with compensation, under certain legal conditions

candidates.label: Property Air Right
candidates.definition: Property Air Rights refer to the legal entitlement to use, develop, or control the space above a piece of real property. This concept extends the traditional notion of property ownership vertically, allowing landowners or developers to potentially construct buildings, sell the rights for development, or otherwise utilize the airspace above their land.

candidates.label: Nonpossessory Interest
candidates.definition: Nonpossessory Interest refers to a type of property interest that grants certain rights to use or enjoy the property without possessing it. This includes interests such as easements, covenants, and profits à prendre, where the holder has limited rights related to the property owned by another person.

candidates.label: Copyright Work Types
candidates.definition: Copyright Work Types encompass various forms of creative works such as Derivative Work, Collective Work, and Joint Work, each with unique characteristics and legal implications. Derivative Works are based on pre-existing works, Collective Works involve the assembly of independent works into a single publication, and Joint Works are created by collaboration with merged contributions.

candidates.label: Title to Property
candidates.definition: Title to property refers to the legal ownership and right to use, possess, and transfer a particular piece of property, whether real estate or personal property. Holding the title signifies that the person or entity has recognized rights and interests in that property, often evidenced by a deed or similar document.

candidates.label: Explicit Agreement
synonyms: An Explicit Agreement is a contract or agreement where the terms and conditions are clearly stated and agreed upon by all parties involved, leaving no room for ambiguity. This type of agreement can be either oral or written, provided the terms are clearly articulated and mutually understood.

candidates.label: Regulatory Document
candidates.definition: A regulatory document is a formal record, report, or submission related to or required by governmental agencies or other regulatory bodies to ensure that companies or organizations comply with applicable laws, rules, and regulations.

candidates.label: Contact Job Title
candidates.definition: Contact Job Title refers to the official designation or position held by an individual who serves as a point of contact for a business or organization. This title provides context for the individual's role and responsibilities, and is often included in professional communications and documentation.

candidates.label: ACH Transfer
candidates.definition: An ACH (Automated Clearing House) Transfer is an electronic method of moving funds between bank accounts via the Automated Clearing House network. Commonly used for domestic transactions, it enables payments like direct deposits and bill payments.

candidates.label: Title Abstract and Settlement Offices
candidates.definition: This U.S. industry comprises establishments (except offices of lawyers and attorneys) primarily engaged in one or more of the following activities: (1) researching public land records to gather information relating to real estate titles; (2) preparing documents necessary for the transfer of the title, financing, and settlement; (3) conducting final real estate settlements and closings; and (4) filing legal and other documents relating to the sale of real estate. Real estate settlement offices, title abstract companies, and title search companies are included in this industry.

candidates.label: Action to Quiet Title
candidates.definition: An Action to Quiet Title is a legal claim brought to establish clear and undisputed ownership of a property, and to remove any clouds or challenges to the title.
synonyms: Action en vue d'obtenir un titre clair

candidates.label: Buyer Recovering Costs Due to Title Defect
candidates.definition: The concept of "Buyer Recovering Costs Due to Title Defect" refers to the legal right of a purchaser of property to seek compensation for expenses incurred as a result of a defect in the title of the property, such as legal fees or costs associated with correcting the defect.
synonyms: Acheteur récupérant les coûts dus à un défaut de titre

candidates.label: Breach of Implied Warranty of Title
candidates.definition: The Breach of Implied Warranty of Title is a legal concept that occurs when a seller of property fails to transfer clear and marketable title to the buyer, despite not making any express warranties about the title.
synonyms: Breach of Implied Warranty of Title
Incumplimiento de garantía implícita de título
Verletzung der stillschweigenden Gewährleistung des Eigentumstitels
Violation de la garantie implicite de titre
Violação da garantia implícita de título

hierarchy_path.label: Breach of Implied Warranty of Title

candidates.label: Condominium Right
candidates.definition: Condominium Right, also known as Horizontal Property Right, refers to the legal structure that allows individual ownership of a unit within a multi-unit building, along with a shared ownership interest in common areas like hallways, elevators, and amenities. This right enables the individual unit owner to sell, lease, or mortgage their unit independently, while being subject to the rules and governance of a homeowners' association or similar entity.

candidates.label: Warranty of Title Clause
candidates.definition: A Warranty of Title Clause is a provision in a contract that guarantees the seller's legal ownership of the property being sold and promises to defend the buyer against any claims made by third parties regarding the title.

candidates.label: Slander of Title
candidates.definition: The term "Slander of Title" refers to a false and malicious statement made about someone's property or ownership rights, which causes harm to their reputation and results in financial loss or damages.

candidates.label: Title to Assets Representation
candidates.definition: Title to Assets Representation is a clause in merger and acquisition agreements where the selling party certifies that the target company holds clear and unencumbered title to its assets. This representation assures the buyer that the assets being acquired are free from liens, claims, encumbrances, or disputes, and that the target company has the full right to transfer them as part of the transaction.

candidates.label: Name Change
candidates.definition: A name change refers to the legal act of altering one's official given name, surname, or both. The process typically requires the submission of a petition to a court and, upon approval, results in a legal decree formally recognizing the new name

candidates.label: Clearly Erroneous
candidates.definition: A legal standard of review used by appellate courts to determine if a lower court's decision was clearly incorrect or mistaken.

candidates.label: Title to Securities Representation
candidates.definition: Title to Securities Representation is a provision in merger and acquisition agreements where the selling party confirms that it holds clear and valid title to the securities being sold or transferred. This representation assures the buyer that the securities in question are free from liens, pledges, encumbrances, or any third-party claims, and that the selling party has the full right to sell or transfer them as part of the transaction.

candidates.label: Clearing Title
candidates.definition: Clearing title refers to the process of resolving legal issues, claims, or encumbrances that could cloud the ownership status or legal rights associated with a particular property or piece of real estate. This process typically involves investigating and addressing any outstanding liens, disputes, or defects in the property's title to establish a clean and unambiguous ownership status. Clearing title is often necessary when buying or selling real estate to ensure that the buyer receives clear and marketable title to the property.

candidates.label: Real Estate Regulatory Services
candidates.definition: Real Estate Regulatory Services refers to legal services related to working with governmental or authorized entities and procedures regarding the monitoring, licensing, and regulating real estate activities within a jurisdiction. These services relate to changes and compliance with laws, rules, and standards related to property transactions, development, and management.

candidates.label: Landlord
candidates.definition: One who lets land to another: the owner or holder of land or houses which he leases or rents to another. Seattle Hous. Auth. v. City of Seattle, 416 P.3d 1280 (Wash. App. 2018)

candidates.label: Tenant
candidates.definition: A person or entity who leases, rents, or occupies property (from a landlord).

candidates.label: Lessor
candidates.definition: A lessor is a person or entity that leases or lets a property to another; a landlord.

candidates.label: Lessee
candidates.definition: A lessee is a person or entity that holds the lease of a property

candidates.label: Holdover Tenant
candidates.definition: A holdover tenant is an individual who remains in a rented property after the expiration of their lease or rental agreement. In such cases, the tenant continues to occupy the property without formal authorization or a renewed agreement with the landlord. Holdover tenancy situations often lead to legal actions, such as eviction proceedings, as the landlord seeks to regain possession of the property.

candidates.label: Subtenant
candidates.definition: A person or entity who subleases property.

candidates.label: Actor / Player
candidates.definition: A person who has a role in a legal matter (e.g., Buyer, Provider, Lawyer, Law Firm, Expert, Employer, Employee, Buyer, Seller, Lessor, Lessee, Debtor, Creditor, Payor, Payee, Landlord, Tenant).

candidates.label: Role in Matter
candidates.definition: An individual or entity's role, responsibility, or function in executing the legal matter.

candidates.label: Landlord Tenant Law
candidates.definition: Law that details the rights and duties of landlords and tenants
synonyms: Direito do locador e do locatário

candidates.label: Real Property Law
candidates.definition: Law that governs the various forms of ownership and tenancy in real property.

candidates.label: Lease Application
candidates.definition: A Lease Application is a formal document submitted by a prospective tenant to a landlord or property management company for the purpose of securing a rental property. The application typically includes personal information, financial data, and references to assess the applicant's suitability.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Lease Abstract
candidates.definition: A Lease Abstract is a summary document that highlights the key terms, conditions, and provisions of a lease agreement. It serves as a quick reference guide for both landlords and tenants, often utilized for portfolio management or due diligence.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Security Deposit
candidates.definition: A security deposit is a sum of money held by a landlord or lessor as collateral to cover potential damages or unpaid rent by a tenant or lessee. It is typically refundable if the terms of the lease are met and no damages occur.

candidates.label: Landlord-Tenant Claims
candidates.definition: Landlord-Tenant Claims refer to legal disputes between a landlord and a tenant, including issues such as failure to return a tenant's security deposit, unlawful eviction, retaliation, and failure to repair or remedy.

candidates.label: Landlord Negligence
candidates.definition: Landlord Negligence refers to a legal claim made by a tenant against their landlord for failing to maintain safe and habitable living conditions on the rental property.

candidates.label: Tenancy Retaliation
candidates.definition: The act of a landlord seeking revenge against a tenant for exercising their legal rights, such as complaining about unsafe living conditions or requesting repairs.

candidates.label: Joint Tenancy
candidates.definition: Joint Tenancy is a form of concurrent ownership where two or more individuals hold equal shares in a property with the right of survivorship. This means that upon the death of one joint tenant, their share automatically passes to the surviving joint tenants.
synonyms: Joint Ownership with Right of Survivorship

candidates.label: Tenancy by the Entirety
candidates.definition: Tenancy by the Entirety is a form of concurrent property ownership available only to married couples, where both spouses hold equal and undivided interest in the property with the right of survivorship. This type of ownership ensures that upon the death of one spouse, the surviving spouse automatically inherits the entire property, and the property cannot be sold or encumbered without the consent of both parties.

candidates.label: Tenancy in Common
candidates.definition: Tenancy in Common is a form of concurrent property ownership where multiple individuals hold separate and undivided interests in the same property. Each tenant in common has the right to sell, transfer, or bequeath their individual share without the consent of the other co-owners, and upon death, their interest passes to their heirs rather than the remaining co-owners.

candidates.label: Landlord's Failure to Protect Tenant from Crime
candidates.definition: The failure of a landlord to provide adequate protection to a tenant from criminal activity, resulting in harm or injury to the tenant.

The application typically includes personal information, financial data, and references to assess the applicant's suitability.

candidates.label: Lease Abstract
candidates.definition: A Lease Abstract is a summary document that highlights the key terms, conditions, and provisions of a lease agreement. It serves as a quick reference guide for both landlords and tenants, often utilized for portfolio management or due diligence.

candidates.label: Security Deposit
candidates.definition: A security deposit is a sum of money held by a landlord or lessor as collateral to cover potential damages or unpaid rent by a tenant or lessee. It is typically refundable if the terms of the lease are met and no damages occur.

candidates.label: Landlord-Tenant Claims
candidates.definition: Landlord-Tenant Claims refer to legal disputes between a landlord and a tenant, including issues such as failure to return a tenant's security deposit, unlawful eviction, retaliation, and failure to repair or remedy.

candidates.label: Landlord Negligence
candidates.definition: Landlord Negligence refers to a legal claim made by a tenant against their landlord for failing to maintain safe and habitable living conditions on the rental property.

candidates.label: Landlord's Failure to Protect Tenant from Crime
candidates.definition: The failure of a landlord to provide adequate protection to a tenant from criminal activity, resulting in harm or injury to the tenant.

candidates.label: Tenancy Retaliation
candidates.definition: The act of a landlord seeking revenge against a tenant for exercising their legal rights, such as complaining about unsafe living conditions or requesting repairs.

candidates.label: Tenancy by the Entirety
candidates.definition: Tenancy by the Entirety is a form of concurrent property ownership available only to married couples, where both spouses hold equal and undivided interest in the property with the right of survivorship. This type of ownership ensures that upon the death of one spouse, the surviving spouse automatically inherits the entire property, and the property cannot be sold or encumbered without the consent of both parties.

candidates.label: Joint Tenancy
candidates.definition: Joint Tenancy is a form of concurrent ownership where two or more individuals hold equal shares in a property with the right of survivorship. This means that upon the death of one joint tenant, their share automatically passes to the surviving joint tenants.
synonyms: Joint Ownership with Right of Survivorship

candidates.label: Tenancy in Common
candidates.definition: Tenancy in Common is a form of concurrent property ownership where multiple individuals hold separate and undivided interests in the same property. Each tenant in common has the right to sell, transfer, or bequeath their individual share without the consent of the other co-owners, and upon death, their interest passes to their heirs rather than the remaining co-owners.

candidates.label: Swaps - Assets
candidates.definition: Swaps are derivative contracts through which two parties exchange financial instruments, typically cash flows or other financial assets, based on predetermined conditions. Common types include interest rate swaps, currency swaps, and commodity swaps, each designed to manage risk or achieve specific financial objectives.

candidates.label: Lease
candidates.definition: A Lease Agreement is a binding contract between a lessor (the owner) and a lessee (the renter) that stipulates the terms and conditions for renting out an asset, such as real estate property or an automobile. The agreement outlines details like the rental amount, lease duration, responsibilities of each party, and other specific provisions related to the rented asset.

candidates.label: Lease Agreements
candidates.definition: A Lease Related Agreement refers to any supplementary contract or document associated with a primary lease agreement, addressing specific terms, conditions, or scenarios that might arise during the lease period. Examples include lease renewals, extensions, terminations, subleases, assignments, and guaranties, among others. These agreements provide clarity and legal framework for particular aspects or contingencies of the lease relationship.

candidates.label: Agreements
candidates.definition: Agreements are written documents that establish a legally binding understanding between two or more parties, outlining the terms and conditions of a transaction or relationship.

candidates.label: Resource Lease
candidates.definition: A Resource Lease is an agreement granting rights to extract natural resources (like minerals, timber, or oil) from a property in exchange for payments, typically royalties based on the amount extracted.

candidates.label: Equipment Agreements
candidates.definition: An Equipment Related Agreement is a legally binding contract that governs the use, rental, purchase, or maintenance of specific equipment or machinery. These agreements detail terms such as ownership, responsibilities for maintenance and repair, payment arrangements, and conditions for returning or replacing the equipment.

candidates.label: Litigation Agreements
candidates.definition: Litigation Related Agreements are contracts associated with disputes, their resolution, and related legal processes. These agreements can dictate the terms, conditions, and procedures to avoid or end litigation.

candidates.label: Equity Agreements
candidates.definition: Equity Related Agreement is an umbrella term for agreements related to ownership interest in a company. It outlines terms and conditions associated with equity ownership, including rights and responsibilities.

candidates.label: Rental Payment
candidates.definition: Rental Payment, also known as Rental Income, refers to the income received by a property owner from tenants in exchange for the use of the property. This income is typically derived from residential, commercial, or industrial real estate and is received on a regular basis, such as monthly or annually.

candidates.label: Contract Formation
candidates.definition: Contract Formation is the event when a contract is formed and fully executed, meeting all legal requirements necessary for it to be binding and enforceable. This includes the elements of offer, acceptance, consideration, mutual assent, and legality of purpose.

candidates.label: Contract Termination
candidates.definition: Contract Termination is an event that occurs when a contract is ended, either at or before its natural expiration date. Termination can be mutual, by breach, or by operation of law.

candidates.label: Lease Obligations
candidates.definition: Lease Obligations refer to the financial and contractual responsibilities that a lessee must fulfill as part of a lease agreement. These often include timely payment of rent, adherence to maintenance and use provisions, and compliance with any terms for lease termination or renewal

candidates.label: Security Deposit
candidates.definition: A security deposit is a sum of money held by a landlord or lessor as collateral to cover potential damages or unpaid rent by a tenant or lessee. It is typically refundable if the terms of the lease are met and no damages occur.

candidates.label: Leasehold Estate
candidates.definition: A Leasehold is an estate in real property where a lessee is granted the exclusive right to occupy and use a property for a predetermined period, typically in exchange for rent or other consideration. Unlike fee simple ownership, leasehold interests are temporary and revert back to the lessor at the end of the lease term

candidates.label: Lease Termination Clause
candidates.definition: A lease termination clause is a provision in a contract that outlines the conditions under which a lease agreement can be ended before its natural expiration date.

candidates.label: Release
candidates.definition: A Release is a legal defense used in civil litigation that involves the relinquishment or surrender of a claim or right by one party to another. A Release can also be a legal document in which one party agrees not to pursue legal action against another party in exchange for compensation or other considerations. It signifies the conclusion of a claim or potential claim.

candidates.label: Lease Practice
candidates.definition: A contract by which one party (landlord, or lessor) gives to another (tenant, or lessee) the use and possession of lands, buildings, property, etc. for a specified time and for fixed payments.

candidates.label: Tenant
candidates.definition: A person or entity who leases, rents, or occupies property (from a landlord).

candidates.label: Landlord
candidates.definition: One who lets land to another: the owner or holder of land or houses which he leases or rents to another. Seattle Hous. Auth. v. City of Seattle, 416 P.3d 1280 (Wash. App. 2018)

candidates.label: Role in Matter
candidates.definition: An individual or entity's role, responsibility, or function in executing the legal matter.

candidates.label: Holdover Tenant
candidates.definition: A holdover tenant is an individual who remains in a rented property after the expiration of their lease or rental agreement. In such cases, the tenant continues to occupy the property without formal authorization or a renewed agreement with the landlord. Holdover tenancy situations often lead to legal actions, such as eviction proceedings, as the landlord seeks to regain possession of the property.

candidates.label: Insurance Claims Form
candidates.definition: An Insurance Claims Form is a standardized document used by a policyholder to request reimbursement or coverage for a loss or event that is insured under their policy. The form usually includes fields for providing details about the claim such as the incident, damages, and other relevant documentation.

candidates.label: Landlord-Tenant Claims
candidates.definition: Landlord-Tenant Claims refer to legal disputes between a landlord and a tenant, including issues such as failure to return a tenant's security deposit, unlawful eviction, retaliation, and failure to repair or remedy.

candidates.label: Landlord Negligence
candidates.definition: Landlord Negligence refers to a legal claim made by a tenant against their landlord for failing to maintain safe and habitable living conditions on the rental property.

candidates.label: Unlawful Eviction of Tenant
candidates.definition: The Unlawful Eviction of Tenant is the act of a landlord removing a tenant from their rental property without following the proper legal procedures, such as obtaining a court order or providing proper notice.
synonyms: 不法なテナント立ち退き

candidates.label: Constructive Eviction of Tenant
candidates.definition: The Constructive Eviction of Tenant is a legal concept that occurs when a landlord's actions or omissions make a rental property uninhabitable, forcing the tenant to move out, and is considered a form of Unlawful Eviction of Tenant.
synonyms: テナントの建設的な立ち退き

candidates.label: Civil Claims
candidates.definition: Civil Claims are legal actions brought by one party against another for a non-criminal matter, such as a breach of contract or personal injury, and seek monetary damages or other relief.

candidates.label: Consumer Contract Claims
candidates.definition: Consumer Contract Claims are legal claims made by a party in a contract with a consumer, including claims related to billing errors, unlawful provisions, unconscionable circumstances, and improper actions by loan brokers.

candidates.label: School Liability Claims
candidates.definition: School Liability Claims: Legal claims brought against educational institutions for damages or injuries caused by the institution's negligence or failure to provide a safe environment for students.

candidates.label: Product Liability Claims
candidates.definition: Product Liability Claims refer to civil tort claims that arise when a consumer is injured or suffers damages due to a defective or dangerous product, and seeks compensation from the manufacturer or seller of the product.

candidates.label: Landlord Indemnification Clause
candidates.definition: A Landlord Indemnification Clause is a specific type of contractual clause that requires the tenant to compensate the landlord for any losses or damages incurred as a result of the tenant's actions or negligence.

candidates.label: Bankruptcy Claims Practice
candidates.definition: Bankruptcy Claims Practice refers to the legal work performed by a Legal Services Provider in the course of a bankruptcy matter, specifically related to the filing, objection, and review of claims.

candidates.label: Claims Filing Practice
candidates.definition: A Claims Filing Practice (Bankruptcy) is a legal practice that involves the preparation and submission of claims in a bankruptcy case.

candidates.label: Real Property Law
candidates.definition: Law that governs the various forms of ownership and tenancy in real property.

candidates.label: Planned Unit Development
candidates.definition: A Planned Unit Development (PUD) is a type of land-use zoning classification that allows for mixed-use residential, commercial, and sometimes industrial areas to be developed according to a single, comprehensive master plan. PUDs often feature more flexible zoning and design standards, aimed at achieving a more integrated and functional community layout.

candidates.label: Farmland
candidates.definition: In legal services, Farmland relates to areas designated for agricultural activities, with specific legal frameworks for land use, agricultural labor, and contractual agreements for sale or lease. Issues can range from zoning laws to crop patents.

candidates.label: Conditional Use Permit
candidates.definition: A Conditional Use Permit is a type of land use authorization that allows a property owner to engage in activities not typically permitted within a specific zoning classification, subject to meeting predetermined conditions. This permit is granted by a local governing body after a review process, often involving public hearings and assessments.

candidates.label: Oregon Land Use Board of Appeals
candidates.definition: The Oregon Land Use Board of Appeals is a specialized court within the Oregon State Courts system that hears appeals related to land use decisions made by local governments.

hierarchy_path.label: Oregon Land Use Board of Appeals

candidates.label: Board of Zoning Appeals
candidates.definition: The Board of Zoning Appeals (BZA) is a specialized governmental body that hears and decides upon appeals, variances, and special exceptions related to local zoning ordinances. Typically quasi-judicial in nature, the BZA reviews cases where applicants seek relief from or challenge the strict application of zoning regulations.

candidates.label: Bureau of Land Management
candidates.definition: The Bureau of Land Management (BLM) is an agency within the United States Department of the Interior that administers public lands in the United States.

candidates.label: Land Subdivision
candidates.definition: This industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in servicing land and subdividing real property into lots, for subsequent sale to builders. Servicing of land may include excavation work for the installation of roads and utility lines. The extent of work may vary from project to project. Land subdivision precedes building activity and the subsequent building is often residential, but may also be commercial tracts and industrial parks. These establishments may do all the work themselves or subcontract the work to others. Establishments that perform only the legal subdivision of land are not included in this industry.

hierarchy_path.label: Heavy and Civil Engineering Construction Industry

candidates.label: Land Dayak languages
candidates.definition: Land Dayak languages are a group of languages spoken by the indigenous Dayak people in Borneo, characterized by their unique grammar and vocabulary.

candidates.label: Zoning Ordinance
candidates.definition: A Zoning Ordinance is a set of legal regulations adopted by municipal or other local government bodies that specifies the types of land use permitted within certain geographic areas. The ordinance usually includes zoning maps and text detailing the land use regulations for different zones, such as residential, commercial, industrial, and other specialized categories.

candidates.label: Land Conservation Rights
candidates.definition: Land Conservation Rights involve the legal entitlements and mechanisms to protect, preserve, and sustainably manage land and its natural resources. This can include the right of individuals, communities, or organizations to conserve land for ecological, historical, recreational, or cultural significance. Additionally, these rights can manifest through tools like conservation easements, land trusts, and zoning regulations, which aim to prevent degradation, limit development, and ensure the long-term protection of the land's intrinsic values.

candidates.label: Abnormal Dangers on Land
candidates.definition: Abnormal Dangers on Land refers to hazardous conditions or situations that exist on a property, which are not typical or expected, and may pose a risk of harm to children who are attracted to the property.

candidates.label: Onshore Wind Energy
candidates.definition: Onshore Wind Energy refers to the generation of electricity through wind turbines located on land rather than offshore locations. It is a key component of renewable energy strategies worldwide, contributing significantly to sustainable energy production.

candidates.label: Land Banking
candidates.definition: Land Banking involves the strategic acquisition and holding of parcels of land by individuals, companies, or government entities with the intent of future sale or development. This practice aims to control the use and development of land, often leading to increased land value over time.

candidates.label: Zoning Text Amendments
candidates.definition: Zoning Text Amendments refer to the changes made to the written regulations and ordinances that define how the land within different zones can be used. These changes can clarify ambiguities, address new development challenges, or enact new policies, and typically require legal review, public hearings, and governmental approvals.

candidates.label: Zoning Map Amendments
candidates.definition: Zoning Map Amendments involve the modification of an official zoning map, which visually represents land-use classifications within a jurisdiction. These changes can shift boundaries, reclassify areas, or add new zones and are generally subjected to public hearings, environmental reviews, and regulatory approvals.

candidates.label: Zoning Document Amendments
candidates.definition: Zoning Document Amendments pertain to the formal changes made to official zoning documents such as zoning maps and zoning ordinance text. These amendments can be initiated by private individuals, developers, or government bodies and usually require a comprehensive legal and regulatory review process, often including public hearings and official approvals.

candidates.label: Density Credit Transactions
candidates.definition: Density Credit Transactions refer to the buying, selling, or transferring of development rights, allowing property owners to adjust the allowable density of development on their land. These transactions are typically facilitated through legal agreements and are overseen by regulatory agencies to ensure compliance with land use and zoning laws.

candidates.label: Use Tax Practice
candidates.definition: Use Tax Practice refers to the application, administration, and enforcement of taxes on the use, storage, or consumption of goods and services that were not subject to sales tax at the time of purchase. These taxes are designed to complement sales taxes and ensure that all taxable transactions are taxed appropriately, even if the purchase occurs out of state or through an online retailer.

In states that recognize land contracts as a form of property ownership, failure to meet the terms of the land contract can result in foreclosure.

candidates.label: Knowledge Representation
candidates.definition: Knowledge Representation is the method of encoding information about the world in a format that an AI system can use to solve complex tasks, including reasoning and inference. This involves using various symbolic or sub-symbolic techniques to model entities, relationships, and rules within a specific domain.

candidates.label: Eminent Domain
candidates.definition: The concept of Eminent Domain (Taking) refers to the power of the government to take private property for public use, with just compensation to the owner, as authorized by the Constitution.

candidates.label: Property Right of Domain
candidates.definition: In civil law jurisdictions, the "Right of Domain" usually refers to the principle that the state has the ultimate ownership over all land within its territory. This concept allows the government to expropriate private property for public use, typically with compensation, under certain legal conditions

candidates.label: De Facto Taking
candidates.definition: The concept of De Facto Taking refers to a situation where the government takes private property without following the proper legal procedures for eminent domain or taking, resulting in the property owner being deprived of their rights without compensation.

candidates.label: Challenging Condemnation
candidates.definition: A legal claim that challenges the government's power to take private property for public use through eminent domain, typically based on constitutional grounds.

candidates.label: Property Right of Habitation
candidates.definition: Right of Habitation

candidates.label: Domain Name Claim
candidates.definition: A domain name claim is a legal assertion by an individual or entity regarding the right to use a specific internet domain name. This claim often arises in the context of trademark disputes, cybersquatting, or domain name registration conflicts.

candidates.label: U.S. Constitutional Claims
candidates.definition: U.S. Constitutional Claims are claims that a person's rights guaranteed by the Constitution have been infringed upon by a government entity or individual acting under the authority of the government.
synonyms: Reclamaciones constitucionales de los EE. UU.
תביעות חוק החוקה של ארצות הברית

candidates.label: Easement Agreement Clause
candidates.definition: A provision in a contract that grants a non-possessory interest in another's land, allowing the holder to use the land for a specific purpose.

candidates.label: Domain Name Registration Process
candidates.definition: The domain name registration process is a series of steps that an individual or entity must follow to acquire a unique internet domain name. This process typically involves choosing a domain name, checking its availability, and registering it with a domain name registrar.

candidates.label: Reverse Mortgage Agreement
candidates.definition: A Reverse Mortgage Agreement is a financial contract that allows homeowners, usually seniors, to convert part of their home equity into cash while retaining ownership of their home. Unlike a traditional mortgage, borrowers do not make monthly payments to the lender; instead, the loan amount, along with interest and fees, is repaid when the homeowner sells the property, moves out permanently, or passes away.

candidates.label: Loan
candidates.definition: A transaction whereby property is lent or given to another on condition of return or, where the loan is of money, repayment. In a loan of money, the money lent becomes the property of the borrower during the period of the loan against an undertaking to return a sum of an equivalent amount either on demand or on a specified date or in accordance with an agreed schedule of repayments.

candidates.label: Interest Payment
candidates.definition: An Interest Payment is a periodic payment made by a borrower to a lender as compensation for the use of borrowed funds. This payment is typically a percentage of the principal amount and is agreed upon at the time of the loan agreement, representing the cost of borrowing money.

candidates.label: U.S. Postal Service
candidates.definition: The United States Postal Service (USPS) is an independent agency of the United States federal government responsible for providing postal service in the United States, including its insular areas and associated states.
synonyms: Servicio Postal de los Estados Unidos
アメリカ合衆国郵便公社

candidates.label: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure is the legal process in which a lender takes possession of a property after the borrower, typically an older homeowner, fails to meet the conditions of the reverse mortgage agreement. This can occur due to reasons like the death of the borrower, failure to pay property taxes or insurance, or permanently moving out of the home. The property is then sold to repay the loan balance and any associated costs

candidates.label: Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: The concept of Mortgage Foreclosure refers to the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property when the borrower fails to make mortgage payments, and sells it to recover the outstanding debt.
synonyms: 抵当フォアクロージャー

candidates.label: Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Foreclosure is the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property, generally after the borrower fails to meet the financial obligations outlined in the mortgage agreement. The process usually culminates in the sale of the property, with the proceeds used to pay off the outstanding debt and any associated costs.

candidates.label: Bilateral Loan
candidates.definition: A Bilateral Loan is a loan agreement between a single borrower and a single lender, typically used for straightforward financing needs. Unlike syndicated loans, bilateral loans involve direct negotiation and agreement on terms between the two parties, offering simplicity and flexibility in loan structuring.

candidates.label: Whole Loan
candidates.definition: a Whole Loan (also known as a Nonsyndicated Loan) refers to a loan or financial arrangement that is provided by a single lender or investor, rather than a group of lenders or investors. This type of arrangement typically involves direct negotiations and agreements between the borrower and the lender.

candidates.label: Bridge Loan
candidates.definition: A Bridge Loan is a short-term financing option used to bridge the gap between the purchase of a new asset and the sale of an existing one. Typically utilized in real estate transactions, bridge loans provide immediate cash flow for the borrower until permanent financing can be arranged or the existing asset is sold.

candidates.label: Mezzanine Loan
candidates.definition: A Mezzanine Loan is a hybrid form of financing that combines debt and equity features, typically used in acquisitions or expansions. It is subordinated to senior debt but ranks higher than equity, often allowing lenders to convert debt into equity if the loan is not repaid on time.

candidates.label: Mortgage Practice
candidates.definition: A transaction in which the owner pledges his/her/its title to real property to a lender as security for a loan described in a promissory note.

candidates.label: Funds Practice
candidates.definition: Funds Practice involves the legal aspects of forming, managing, and regulating investment funds, including private equity funds, hedge funds, and venture capital funds. This practice area covers fund structuring, compliance with financial regulations, investor relations, and transactional activities.

candidates.label: Tax Practice
candidates.definition: Tax practice refers lawyers assisting individuals or organizations in navigating tax laws, including compliance, filing returns, and handling disputes with tax authorities. These lawyers may also advise on tax planning strategies to optimize tax liability and benefit

candidates.label: M&A F8 Tax Practice
candidates.definition: M&A F8 Tax Practice refers to the specialized legal services provided by a legal services provider in the area of tax law related to mergers and acquisitions within the M&A F Regulatory and Specialty Matters Practice.
synonyms: Práctica fiscal de fusiones y adquisiciones F8
